source.title: Impact of treatment discontinuation among patients with microsatellite instability or mismatch repair-deficient metastatic colorectal cancer treated with immune checkpoint inhibitors.

finding.description: 51% of patients stopped treatment due to disease progression and 49% discontinued for other reasons.

source_quote: After a median follow-up period of 48 months, 51% of patients stopped treatment due to disease progression and 49% discontinued for other reasons, including immune-related adverse events, clinical benefit, protocol completion (2 years), surgery, or pregnancy.

source.title: Impact of treatment discontinuation among patients with microsatellite instability or mismatch repair-deficient metastatic colorectal cancer treated with immune checkpoint inhibitors.

finding.description: Among patients who stopped per protocol, nearly 80% had negative ctDNA at discontinuation, with only 7% of them progressing during follow-up.

source_quote: Among those who stopped per protocol, nearly 80% had negative ctDNA at discontinuation, with only 7% of them progressing during follow-up.

source.title: Impact of treatment discontinuation among patients with microsatellite instability or mismatch repair-deficient metastatic colorectal cancer treated with immune checkpoint inhibitors.

finding.description: Among patients who progressed, 78.3% had positive liquid biopsy results at treatment discontinuation.

source_quote: Conversely, among those patients who progressed, 78.3% had positive liquid biopsy results at treatment discontinuation, underscoring the complexity of molecular monitoring.

source.title: Impact of treatment discontinuation among patients with microsatellite instability or mismatch repair-deficient metastatic colorectal cancer treated with immune checkpoint inhibitors.

finding.description: The mPFS for the entire cohort was 10.8 months (95% CI 7.3 months, upper limit not reached).

source_quote: With a median follow-up of 48 months (95% CI 42.1-50.7 months), the mPFS for the entire cohort was 10.8 months (95% CI 7.3 months, upper limit not reached [NR]).

source.title: Impact of treatment discontinuation among patients with microsatellite instability or mismatch repair-deficient metastatic colorectal cancer treated with immune checkpoint inhibitors.

finding.description: In the multivariable analysis, the number of previous treatment lines and ECOG performance status were associated with PFS (HR 0.43, 95% CI 0.22-0.88, P = 0.020 and HR 3.18, 95% CI 1.58-6.40, P = 0.001, respectively).

source_quote: In the multivariable analysis, the number of previous treatment lines and ECOG performance status were associated with PFS (HR 0.43, 95% CI 0.22-0.88, P = 0.020 and HR 3.18, 95% CI 1.58-6.40, P = 0.001, respectively).

source.title: Impact of treatment discontinuation among patients with microsatellite instability or mismatch repair-deficient metastatic colorectal cancer treated with immune checkpoint inhibitors.

finding.description: The mOS was 66.2 months (95% CI 37.5-NR months).

source_quote: In terms of OS, the median overall survival (mOS) was 66.2 months (95% CI 37.5-NR months).

source.title: Impact of treatment discontinuation among patients with microsatellite instability or mismatch repair-deficient metastatic colorectal cancer treated with immune checkpoint inhibitors.

finding.description: In the multivariate analysis, only the number of previous treatment lines (0-1 versus >1) and ECOG performance status (0 vs ≥1) were associated with OS (HR 0.29, 95% CI 0.11-0.77, P = 0.011, and HR 4.50, 95% CI 1.95-10.41, P < 0.001, respectively).

source_quote: In the multivariate analysis, only the number of previous treatment lines (0-1 versus >1) and ECOG performance status (0 vs ≥1) were associated with OS (HR 0.29, 95% CI 0.11-0.77, P = 0.011, and HR 4.50, 95% CI 1.95-10.41, P < 0.001, respectively).

source.title: Impact of treatment discontinuation among patients with microsatellite instability or mismatch repair-deficient metastatic colorectal cancer treated with immune checkpoint inhibitors.

finding.description: European Society for Medical Oncology and National Comprehensive Cancer Network guidelines recommend treatment with anti-PD-1 or anti-PD-1/anti-CTLA-4 as the preferred first-line option in MSI mCRC.
finding.guideline_body: European Society for Medical Oncology and National Comprehensive Cancer Network

source_quote: Thus, European Society for Medical Oncology and National Comprehensive Cancer Network guidelines recommend treatment with anti-PD-1 or anti-PD-1/anti-CTLA-4 as the preferred first-line option in MSI mCRC.
